1: Comparative life cycle assessment of technologies and strategies to improve nitrogen use efficiency in egg supply chains
Shiva Zargar Ershadi a, , Mohammad Davoud Heidari a Baishali Dutta a , Goretty Dias b , Nathan Pelletier a

<list>a 340 Fipke Centre for Innovative Research, University of British Columbia, Okanagan, 3247 University Way, Kelowna, V1V 1V7, Canada 
b School of Environment, Enterprise and Development, University of Waterloo, 200 University Avenue West, Waterloo, N2L 3G1, Canada</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Eggs Canada Life cycle assessment Nitrogen use efficiency Management strategy Mitigation technology|"A non-trivial challenge along egg supply chains is inefficient use of nitrogen, which may have a combination of negative economic, human/animal health, and environmental implications. A variety of technologies and management strategies have been proposed to improve nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) and reduce emissions at key supply chain stages. This study considered seven scenarios representing NUE strategies: (1) biochar addition to the soil and (2) application of the ""4Rs"" approach to fertilizer management in feed input production; (3) a reduced crude protein supplemented with synthetic amino acids diet; (4) use of acid scrubbers in poultry barns; (5) biochar addition to stored manure; (6) manure incorporation at the time of land application; and (7) joint application of all strategies. ISO 14044 compliant environmental life cycle assessment (LCA) along with NUE calculations were performed to analyze and compare these mitigation options. The functional unit was one tonne of egg production at the farm gate in Canada. The most significant increase in NUE (13%) resulted from the application of the scrubber in the layer barn. The scrubber also significantly lowered acidifying (21%) and eutrophying (16%) emissions compared to the baseline. The combined application of all strategies increased NUE by 15% compared to the baseline scenario and reduced acidification, global warming, and eutrophication po- tential, but at the cost of a large increase in energy consumption. Each strategy might be more or less suitable depending on the considered environmental impacts, as well as NUE outcomes. Use of LCA is essential to informed decision making in this context."
2: Food production is responsible for a substantial share of anthropo- genic resource consumption and environmental emissions (Pelletier and Tyedmers 2010; Pelletier et al., 2018), including reactive nitrogen (N) emissions (Leinonen et al., 2012a). Various Nr species, including ammonia (NH3), nitrous oxide (N20), nitrogen oxides (NO), and nitrate (NO), are lost from agricultural systems - in particular, from livestock production and production of feeds consumed by livestock. These emissions variously contribute to GHG emissions, acidification, eutro- phication, biodiversity losses, and soil, water, and air pollution (Groe- nestein et al., 2019).

The egg industry is the fastest-growing livestock sector. Global egg production increased from 51 million tonnes of eggs in 2000 to 68 million tonnes in 2013 (FAO 2016). Like other livestock sectors, the Canadian egg industry is under increasing pressure to demonstrate

continued progress with respect to sustainability objectives. Improving nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) has been identified as one of the top sustainability priorities for this sector (Gerber et al., 2014; Pelletier 2017; Pelletier et al., 2018).

NUE is a measure of the ratio between the amount of N outputs and inputs associated with provision of a good or service (Brentrap and Palliere 2010). NUE plays a key role in determining the egg industry's net nitrogen footprint balance, as well as impacts on air quality, human and animal health, and profitability (Pelletier et al., 2018). NUE is influenced by the production and composition of feeds, feed conversion efficiency, and manure management in barns, storage and land appli- cation (Pelletier et al., 2018; Zargar et al., 2020).

A variety of technological and management strategies may reduce nitrogen losses at these key points along egg supply chains (Pelletier et al., 2018; Zargar et al., 2020), but available literature does not pro- vide clear direction with respect to their comparative efficacy and

feasibility. To remedy this knowledge gap and to identify a priority subset that should be subjected to further comparative, quantitative analysis, Zargar et al. (2020) applied a set of technical, economic, and environmental efficacy criteria to screen and short-list available strat- egies/technologies. These criteria were selected based on analysis of various literature sources, including (Samah et al. 2010; Amer and Daim 2011; Ahmad and Tahar 2014; Zaini et al. 2015; Aydiner et al. 2016; Lijó et al. 2018). They were used to characterize and compare available mitigation options at each focal stage of the crop-livestock supply chain. With respect to environmental criteria, only technologies/strategies that were reported to produce non-trivial net emissions reduction benefits for the major agricultural reactive N species were considered. For example, a technology that was reported to reduce ammonia emissions by 30% but increase nitrous oxide emissions by 50%, was not further considered. Similarly, only those technologies/strategies explicitly described in the literature as technically feasible for farmers to implement and maintain were short-listed for quantitative evaluation. Finally, the tech- nology/strategy had to be reported to be no more than moderately expensive to install, operate and maintain. The latter is quite important since farmers are likely to only adopt cost-effective mitigation options. On this basis, the following subset was identified: implementation of the "4Rs" (Right Source @ Right Rate @ Right Time @ Right Place approach and biochar addition to cropland for the feed input production stage; reduced crude protein content in formulated feeds supplemented with synthetic amino acids for the feed formulation stage; use of ammonia scrubbers in barns; biochar addition to the stored manure; and manure incorporation at the time of land application for the manure management stage. A number of other technologies and strategies such as manure belts and roofed storage were also found to satisfy all of the aforementioned criteria. However, since these are already widely applied in the Canadian egg industry, further quantitative evaluation was not indicated.

However, it is imperative that the implementation of any such stra- tegies be informed by a broader, systems level analysis and comparison of their relative efficacy with respect to NUE, and impacts on the overall environmental sustainability of egg production (Lemmen and Warren 2004; Pelletier et al., 2018). Life cycle assessment (LCA) provides an effective means to consider the relative efficacy of each N emissions mitigation option and to quantify NUE in parallel to a variety of other resource and environmental outcomes (Roy et al., 2009; Erisman et al., 2018). Such an approach is essential to understand and prevent burden-shifting among supply chain stages, different stakeholders and kinds of impacts (Williams et al., 2011; Pelletier 2015). Several studies have used the LCA methodology to evaluate environmental burdens of egg supply chains (Mollenhorst et al., 2006; Cederberg et al., 2009; Vergé et al., 2009; Wiedemann and McGahan 2011; Dekker et al., 2011; Leinonen et al., 2012b; Leinonen and Kyriazakis 2013; Pelletier et al., 2013; Pelletier 2017, 2018; Abín et al., 2018). However, none of these studies assessed the comparative efficacy of alternative technologies and management strategies for nitrogen emissions reduction along egg supply chains.

The objective of the current study was hence to provide information that will enable improving NUE in the egg sector whilst simultaneously considering other resource and environmental impacts. Towards the end, a series of life cycle inventory (LCI) models were developed for the priority subset of mitigation technologies and management strategies identified by Zargar et al. (2020). These models were integrated into an LCI model of the Canadian egg industry (Pelletier 2017) to evaluate their comparative efficacy.

7: To assess the comparative efficacy of N1 emissions mitigation options for egg supply chains identified by Zargar et al. (2020) with respect to

NUE as well as resource/environmental criteria, an ISO 14044-compliant LCA was conducted. The research methods are described in correspondence with the four phases of LCA (ISO 2006a, b), as per the ISO 14044 methodological standard.
8: The overarching goal of this study was to evaluate and compare a subset of NUE technologies and management strategies that might be applied along egg supply chains to determine their comparative efficacy in simultaneously reducing Nr emissions, resource use, and other envi- ronmental impacts, as well as to elucidate potential trade-offs. Towards this end, from 2019 to 2020 a series of LCI models were developed and integrated into the Canadian egg supply chain model developed by Pelletier (2017).

Attributional life cycle assessment (ALCA) modeling was applied. The use of ALCA supports the understanding of the current distribution of potential environmental burdens along product supply chains. The intended audience includes egg farmers and egg industry associations, producers of feed inputs for the poultry industry, government agencies tasked with regulating agriculture, and agroecosystem modeling experts.
9: <header>2.1.2. Description of the conventional egg supply chain in Canada </header>

The baseline conventional cage Canadian egg supply chain model described by Pelletier (2017) has been adopted for this study as this currently represents the majority of egg production in Canada. Con- ventional cage systems for layer hens typically include automatic drinking, feeding, egg collection, and manure removal systems in tunnel-ventilated barns. Manure falls through the wire cage floor onto a belt, or into a pit for disposal. Hens are moved into the cages as pullets at roughly 20 weeks of age, and spend 50 weeks in the cages before being cycled out of production. During this time, they are fed a diet of domestically produced, formulated feed. The laying hens will produce roughly 300 eggs each during their lay cycle (Pelletier 2017).
10: To calculate reactive nitrogen emissions for the baseline and each alternative scenario, as well as any associated differences in NUE for the activity and for egg production as a whole, Eqs. (1) and (2) were applied. N input - N output NUE = 100 (1) N input Nitrogen footprint = Nr output (2)

At the crop production stage, NUE was calculated based on one tonne of produced crop. For corn and wheat production, the total amount of nitrogen fertilizer applied per tonne of crop was considered as the N input. For soybean production, the N input comprised of a small amount of nitrogen fertilizer and biological N2 fixation. An N2 fixation rate of 202 kg N ha was assumed based on (Ciampitti and Salvagiotti 2018). The N output was the N fraction of all N emissions. N footprints included all N emissions, expressed in kg Nr.

For the feed formulation stage, NUE was calculated based on two tonnes of feed consumed (i.e. per one tonne of eggs produced). N inputs consisted of total N in the feed. Outputs were N resident in manure/ mortalities and spent hens (here, emissions are the difference between N inputs in feeds and N retained in the co-products of egg production).

For the manure management stages, the first step was calculating the amount of N excreted in the barn, which was considered as the N input for the barn stage of manure management. The N output consists of N remaining in the manure, following Nr emissions in the barn. The remaining N in the manure was the N input to the subsequent manure storage stage and similarly through manure land application.
For the entire egg production supply chain, NUE was calculated for one tonne of egg production. N inputs and outputs were calculated as follows:
N input: N fertilizer applied to produce corn, wheat, soymeal, and canola oil as well as N2 fixed by soybeans and N contained in synthetic amino acids.

N retained: N in one tonne of eggs + N in manure at the time of field application after emissions + N in valorized spent hens + N credit from the ammonia captured by the acid scrubber (assuming it is utilized as a fertilizer product).
11: To calculate reactive nitrogen emissions for the baseline and each alternative scenario, as well as any associated differences in NUE for the activity and for egg production as a whole, Eqs. (1) and (2) were applied. N input - N output NUE = 100 (1) N input Nitrogen footprint = Nr output (2)

At the crop production stage, NUE was calculated based on one tonne of produced crop. For corn and wheat production, the total amount of nitrogen fertilizer applied per tonne of crop was considered as the N input. N footprints included all N emissions, expressed in kg Nr.

For the feed formulation stage, NUE was calculated based on two tonnes of feed consumed (i.e. per one tonne of eggs produced). N inputs consisted of total N in the feed. Outputs were N resident in manure/ mortalities and spent hens (here, emissions are the difference between N inputs in feeds and N retained in the co-products of egg production).

For the manure management stages, the first step was calculating the amount of N excreted in the barn, which was considered as the N input for the barn stage of manure management. The N output consists of N remaining in the manure, following Nr emissions in the barn. The remaining N in the manure was the N input to the subsequent manure storage stage and similarly through manure land application.
For the entire egg production supply chain, NUE was calculated for one tonne of egg production. N inputs and outputs were calculated as follows:
N input: N fertilizer applied to produce corn, wheat, soymeal, and canola oil as well as N2 fixed by soybeans and N contained in synthetic amino acids.

N retained: N in one tonne of eggs + N in manure at the time of field application after emissions + N in valorized spent hens + N credit from the ammonia captured by the acid scrubber (assuming it is utilized as a fertilizer product).
12: To calculate reactive nitrogen emissions for the baseline and each alternative scenario, as well as any associated differences in NUE for the activity and for egg production as a whole, Eqs. (1) and (2) were applied. N input - N output NUE = 100 (1) N input Nitrogen footprint = Nr output (2)

At the crop production stage, NUE was calculated based on one tonne of produced crop. For corn and wheat production, the total amount of nitrogen fertilizer applied per tonne of crop was considered as the N input. For soybean production, the N input comprised of a small amount of nitrogen fertilizer and biological N2 fixation. An N2 fixation rate of 202 kg N ha was assumed based on (Ciampitti and Salvagiotti 2018). The N output was the N fraction of all N emissions. N footprints included all N emissions, expressed in kg Nr.

For the feed formulation stage, NUE was calculated based on two tonnes of feed consumed (i.e. per one tonne of eggs produced). N inputs consisted of total N in the feed. Outputs were N resident in manure/ mortalities and spent hens (here, emissions are the difference between N inputs in feeds and N retained in the co-products of egg production).

For the manure management stages, the first step was calculating the amount of N excreted in the barn, which was considered as the N input for the barn stage of manure management. The N output consists of N remaining in the manure, following Nr emissions in the barn. The remaining N in the manure was the N input to the subsequent manure storage stage and similarly through manure land application.
For the entire egg production supply chain, NUE was calculated for one tonne of egg production. N inputs and outputs were calculated as follows:
N input: N fertilizer applied to produce corn, wheat, soymeal, and canola oil as well as N2 fixed by soybeans and N contained in synthetic amino acids.

N retained: N in one tonne of eggs + N in manure at the time of field application after emissions + N in valorized spent hens + N credit from the ammonia captured by the acid scrubber (assuming it is utilized as a fertilizer product).
13: The system boundary is defined to clarify what is included/excluded within the model (ISO 2006a, b). The system boundary for this study followed that defined by Pelletier (2017) up to the farm gate, including inputs (materials, energy, and transportation) and emissions charac- teristic of industry average breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, and layer facilities. This boundary was subsequently expanded to include the in- ventories and upstream supply chains of each of the NUE tech- nology/management strategies considered. The technologies include: the 4Rs approach to fertilizer management and biochar addition to the soil at the feed input production stage; a reduced crude protein diet supplemented with synthetic amino acids at the feed formulation stage; and use of an acid scrubber, biochar addition to the stored manure and incorporation at the barn, storage and land application stages of manure management, respectively. Emissions from manure were considered to be emitted from the barn, during storage and at the time of land appli- cation. The system boundaries are illustrated in Fig. 1.
14: The selection of an appropriate functional unit (FU) to which the LCI is scaled is essential to characterize the resource and environmental performance of a product system (Reap et al., 2008). The FU/reference flow for this study was one tonne of eggs produced at the farm gate.
15: <header>2.1.6. Life cycle inventory analysis </header>

Foreground LCI model inputs and outputs, including direct, and in- direct emissions considered in this study included those attributable to the production and processing of feed inputs and other raw materials, energy carriers, and manure management from the baseline model, along with those specific to each of the mitigation options considered.

2.1.6.1. Data acquisition, assumptions, and limitations. Required data to develop LCI models for each mitigation strategy were collected from literature, databases, industry publications, catalogues, consultancy re- ports, and via personal consultation with domain experts in related companies and are summarized in the following sub sections. Tables reporting detailed LCI data are provided in Appendix A.

2.1.6.1.1. Feed input production. The primary inventory data for feed input production (corn, soybean, and wheat, which comprise the majority share of layer feed in Canada) were based on Pelletier (2017). The mitigation options considered were the application of 4Rs fertilizer management and biochar addition to the soil.

2.1.6.1.2. 4Rs fertilizer management. The inventory data for the 4Rs strategy were derived from (Li et al., 2016). Li et al. (2016) indicated that an 11% reduction in N fertilizer use is achievable while maintaining corn yield via a sensor-based variable-rate N application in Lincoln County, MO. The fertilizer flow in the baseline model (Pelletier 2017)

was modified based on Li et al. (2016) to represent the 4Rs approach. The 4Rs technique may also reduce the emissions of nitrous oxide, ammonia, and nitrate emissions in corn production by 10, 23, and 16%, respectively (Li et al., 2016). There was no literature found reporting the amount of fertilizer and emissions reduction associated with the appli- cation of the 4Rs approach in wheat and soybean production, specif- ically. Hence, the same% N fertilizer usage and emissions reduction that has been reported for corn was applied to the fertilizer usage and emissions for wheat and soybean production as calculated for the re- gions in which they produced in Canada.
The calculation of emissions reduction utilized Eq. (3): X = Z%) + Y (3)

<list>X= the amount of emission after application of mitigation option 
Y= the amount of emission before application of mitigation option 
Z= the percentage of reduction 
2.1.6.1.3. Biochar addition to soil. The primary data for biochar production were derived from Gu and Bergman (2016), who evaluated the co-production of biochar and syngas using a modular advanced biomass pyrolysis system in the US. The data were modified to best reflect Canadian conditions. The feedstock was assumed to be wood- chips from BC forests or mill residues. It was assumed that the biochar was produced in BC and shipped to the provinces producing the considered crops (corn, wheat, and soybean). 
Prior research has shown that the choice of system boundaries for biochar production models can significantly influence estimated life cycle impacts in particular with respect to allocation between syngas and biochar co-products. Consensus as to best practice has not yet coalesced (Matustík et al., 2020). Two scenarios were hence imple- mented in which biochar production was modeled using either physical allocation based on the energy content of biochar and syngas, or using a system expansion in which syngas was assumed to displace propane at a conversion rate of 78% (Ge et al., 2008). 
The application rate of biochar to agricultural soils depends on soil and crop type. According to literature, biochar application rates may vary from 5 to 50 tonnes per hectare (Chan et al., 2007; Major 2009, 2010). Some studies suggest that higher rates of biochar negatively affected crop yield. In the current study, a sensitivity analysis was conducted, and three rates of biochar addition (5, 7.5, and 10 tonnes per hectare) were modelled based on Dennis (2019). Due to the dependency of N emissions on application rate, type of soil, type of crop etc. (He et al., 2017) as well as lack of clarity with respect to the nature of this relationship (linear or otherwise), it was assumed that the lowest rate of biochar application modelled in this research (5 tonne/ha) has the lowest percentage of emission reduction from the range reported in Zargar et al. (2020). For example, biochar addition to the soil has been reported to reduce N2O emissions by 25-50%. Therefore, the following assumptions were made: 5, 7.5 and 10 tonne/ha biochar addition to the soil reduced N2O by 25%, 37.5%, and 50%, respectively. NO3 (0-25%) and NO (50-75%) emissions were reduced based on the same assump- tions. The amount of N20, NO, and NO3 in the baseline model, simulated using IPCC tier 2 protocols, were modified based on the above as- sumptions to reflect the anticipated impact of biochar application on N emissions. It was assumed biochar was incorporated into agricultural soils since the majority of biochar field trials reported used this appli- cation method (Major 2010). 
Biochar effects on soil and crop productivity cannot be generalized, as they depend on the type of biochar and plant as well as the site characteristics (Lorenz and Lal 2014). Jeffery et al. (2011) demonstrated that there is no correlation between the biochar application rate and the impacts on crop productivity. However, Gryze et al., 2010 found that biochar application reduced fertilizer application by 7% as a result of increased nutrient availability. Hence, a 7% fertilizer application rate reduction was considered when biochar was applied. 
Biochar is also advantageous in its potential for carbon sequestration that removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere as a result of storing</list>

carbon in soil organic matter. Windeatt et al. (2014) reported a potential of 373 Mt of biochar to sequester 0.55 Pg CO2 per year in soils over long-time periods. On this basis, it was assumed that 36 kg of biochar has an associated 53.01 kg annual carbon sequestration potential.

Another key feature of biochar is its resistance to decomposition in soils. This allows for a single application of biochar to be useful over several growing seasons (Major 2010). However, there is no recom- mended application frequency since research on biochar is relatively new/limited (Dennis 2019). The few studies that have been conducted to determine biochar application frequency best practices suggest application every three years (Backer et al., 2016; Bieser and Thomas 2019). Therefore, the frequency of application was considered to be three years in the current study as well.

The LCI data utilized to model feed input production with the application of the 4Rs approach and biochar addition to the soil are provided in Tables A.1 to A.3 for corn, soybean, and wheat production, respectively.

2.1.6.1.4. Feed formulation. Feed formulation is a critical and com- plex process to meet the nutritional requirements of laying hens so as to ensure productivity in a cost-effective manner (Moe et al., 2014). To evaluate the potential of improving NUE through feed formulation, a feed with reduced crude protein and supplemental amino acids was compared to a typical layer feed in Canada. Here, two representative formulations (a baseline and an alternative, reduced crude protein feed formulation with synthetic amino acids supplementation) were solicited from a western Canadian feed milling company (pers. comm, Alizadeh, 2019, Otter Coop) (Table A.4).

LCI models and source databases used to model ingredients in the feed formulations that were not otherwise provided in Pelletier (2017) are provided in Table A.5. Feed inputs affect nutrient excretion and associated emissions from manure; therefore, detailed feed formulations were provided in order to increase the transparency and accessibility of available data to other researchers to make comparisons among various diets. LCI data for DL-Methionine, L-lysine, L-threonine, monocalcium phosphate, and sodium bicarbonate were derived from AGRIBALYSE V1.3 2016), based on the project ECOALIM. Premix was modelled based on information provided by Otter Coop (pers. comm, Alizadeh, 2019, Otter Coop). Liquid choline was excluded from the analysis due to lack of reliable inventory data for the production process. As liquid choline is included at the same rate in both formulations, this omission is not anticipated to unduly influence the analysis.

2.1.6.1.5. Manure management. The manure management stage consists of three sub-stages: manure management in the barn, during storage, and at the time of land application. The baseline model (Pel- letier, 2017) consists of the technologies and management strategies that are already widely applied in the Canadian egg industry, some of which were shown in Zargar et al. (2020) to be effective in reducing N emissions compared to alternative strategies. These mitigation options include mechanical ventilation and use of manure belts in barns, use of roofed manure storage, and spreading the manure on land. However, other strategies were identified by Zargar et al. (2020), such as use of acid scrubbers at the barn stage, biochar addition to the stored manure, and incorporating manure at the time of land application. All were seen as potentially efficacious options to further reduce N emissions. Hence, a comparison among baseline versus alternative mitigation options were conducted at each sub stage of manure management. Specifically, for manure management in the barn, application of a manure belt and mechanical ventilation was considered as the baseline model, and an ammonia scrubber was applied as an additional, alternative option. Reported amounts of acid, electricity and water used in acid scrubbers, as well as the type of acid employed, vary considerably. As a result, a sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate and compare the extent to which the amount of inputs influences the estimated life cycle impacts. The most common type of acid used in the acid scrubber is sulfuric acid, which was considered in all models. Separate LCIs for acid scrubbers were considered based on (Hadlocon et al., 2015a), (Zhao et al., 2013),
and Zhang et al. (2016a), as well an average of these three studies. The results of the sensitivity analysis are provided in section 3.7.

It was assumed that the scrubber removed 75% of ammonia from ventilation air based on the low end of the range for ammonia removal efficiency (75-100%) reported in the literature (Carew 2010; Lin et al., 2014; Hadlocon et al. 2015; Wood and Van Heyst 2016). Based on an estimated N excretion rate by hens of 32.18 kg N/tonne of eggs and assuming 50% of ammonia emissions from the barn and storage occur in the barn, the total amount of N scrubbed was calculated accordingly. The captured NH3 was assumed to be made available as a fertilizer input to crop production, thereby displacing production of an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. LCI data for manure management in the barn with and without the application of a scrubber are provided in Appendix A.3 (LCI is provided in Table A.6).

At the manure storage stage, roofed storage was considered as the baseline model. Biochar addition to the stored manure was considered as an alternative strategy with the same LCI data as previously described for crop production. The rate of biochar application varies according to manure properties and feedstock materials (Steiner et al., 2010; Kalus et al., 2019). For the current analysis, a rate of 5% of poultry litter by mass was assumed based on (Steiner et al., 2010). LCI data for manure management in storage is provided in Table A.7. Biochar addition to manure reduced emissions (ammonia and nitrous oxide by the average amount of 62.5% and 87.5%, respectively), resulting in more N-rich manure at the time of land application.

At the manure land application stage, manure spreading was applied in the baseline model. Incorporating manure was considered as an alternative strategy. LCI data for manure land application is provided in Table A.8. Incorporation was assumed to be via chisel plow based on (Mark Hanna et al., 2014).

The previously described LCIs for the manure management stage were subsequently used in combination to model the potential combined effects of applying the strategies (Table A.9). The LCI models, including all background inventories as well as data quality scores, for these an- alyses are available as a Zolca file on the Open Science Framework at htt ps://osf.io/p2gmn/.

2.1.6.1.6. Egg production. LCI data, models, and source databases used to model production of one tonne of eggs assuming application of all mitigation strategies are provided in Table A.10. The feed production and manure management processes were modified, considering the application of the various strategies.

2.1.6.2. Modeling N P, and methane emissions from poultry manure in the barn, storage, and land application stages. The application of suitable emissions models is crucial to increase the reliability of LCIA results (Zargar submitted). Here, suitable emissions models for estimating N emissions were determined based on (Zargar et al., submitted), which reviewed, characterized, and compared publicly available N emission models for use in LCA. Among the 48 models reviewed, IPCC Tier 2 emission models were identified as the most suitable option for the current study, due to their capacity to support simulating nitrous oxide, ammonia, nitrate, and nitric oxide emissions at the national scale (which is consistent with the application context of the current study). The IPCC methods are intended to support quantification of GHG emissions, including from both direct and indirect emissions of nitrous oxide. In- direct sources of nitrous oxide emissions occur via ammonia, nitrate, and nitric oxide volatilization, deposition, leaching and runoff. Hence, the IPCC methods also provide methods and emission factors for calculating emissions of ammonia, nitrate, and nitric oxide (IPCC, 2006), which were used in the current analysis.

The rate of nitrogen excretion by poultry is required to calculate the amount of emissions in the barn, storage, and land application stages of manure management. Nitrogen excretion rates were calculated as described in Pelletier (2017) via a nutrient mass balance model, with feed input nitrogen contents derived from www.feedipedia.org. The

proportion of emitted nitrous oxide, ammonia and nitric oxide from the nitrogen excreted in barn VS. storage stages is unclear. IPCC Tier 2 provides emission factors for the combination of these two stages only. To consider barn and manure storage stages separately, it was assumed that emissions were divided equally between these two stages (i.e. 50% of total emissions were attributed to the barn stage and the other 50% to the storage stage). A sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate the influence of this assumption on model results assuming a 25%/75% split, and vice versa.

Emissions from manure land application were assessed using IPCC (2006) Tier 2 default emission factors. The phosphorus excretion rate was calculated via a nutrient mass balance. Phosphorus emissions were calculated based on an assumed 2.9% run-off rate at the time of manure application (Dalgaard et al., 2008). Methane emissions were assumed to be emitted 100% in manure storage, and were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 2 methods.

2.1.6.3. Uncertainty assessment. To quantify uncertainty related to sto- chastic variability and data quality, a pedigree matrix was applied using the Ecoinvent data quality system in openLCA. Pedigree scores were applied to all foreground and background systems LCI data points. Monte Carlo (MC) simulations (1000 runs) were subsequently per- formed using openLCA software to quantify uncertainties for the LCIA results (standard error).

The application of suitable emissions models is crucial to increase the reliability of LCIA results (Zargar submitted). Here, suitable emissions models for estimating N emissions were determined based on (Zargar et al., submitted), which reviewed, characterized, and compared publicly available N emission models for use in LCA. Among the 48 models reviewed, IPCC Tier 2 emission models were identified as the most suitable option for the current study, due to their capacity to support simulating nitrous oxide, ammonia, nitrate, and nitric oxide emissions at the national scale (which is consistent with the application context of the current study). The IPCC methods are intended to support quantification of GHG emissions, including from both direct and indirect emissions of nitrous oxide. In- direct sources of nitrous oxide emissions occur via ammonia, nitrate, and nitric oxide volatilization, deposition, leaching and runoff. Hence, the IPCC methods also provide methods and emission factors for calculating emissions of ammonia, nitrate, and nitric oxide (IPCC, 2006), which were used in the current analysis.

The rate of nitrogen excretion by poultry is required to calculate the amount of emissions in the barn, storage, and land application stages of manure management. Nitrogen excretion rates were calculated as described in Pelletier (2017) via a nutrient mass balance model, with feed input nitrogen contents derived from www.feedipedia.org. The

proportion of emitted nitrous oxide, ammonia and nitric oxide from the nitrogen excreted in barn VS. storage stages is unclear. IPCC Tier 2 provides emission factors for the combination of these two stages only. To consider barn and manure storage stages separately, it was assumed that emissions were divided equally between these two stages (i.e. 50% of total emissions were attributed to the barn stage and the other 50% to the storage stage). A sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate the influence of this assumption on model results assuming a 25%/75% split, and vice versa.

Emissions from manure land application were assessed using IPCC (2006) Tier 2 default emission factors. The phosphorus excretion rate was calculated via a nutrient mass balance. Phosphorus emissions were calculated based on an assumed 2.9% run-off rate at the time of manure application (Dalgaard et al., 2008). Methane emissions were assumed to be emitted 100% in manure storage, and were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 2 methods.

2.1.6.3. Uncertainty assessment. To quantify uncertainty related to sto- chastic variability and data quality, a pedigree matrix was applied using the Ecoinvent data quality system in openLCA. Pedigree scores were applied to all foreground and background systems LCI data points. Monte Carlo (MC) simulations (1000 runs) were subsequently per- formed using openLCA software to quantify uncertainties for the LCIA results (standard error).
17: <header>2.1.6. Life cycle inventory analysis </header>

Foreground LCI model inputs and outputs, including direct, and in- direct emissions considered in this study included those attributable to the production and processing of feed inputs and other raw materials, energy carriers, and manure management from the baseline model, along with those specific to each of the mitigation options considered.

2.1.6.1. Data acquisition, assumptions, and limitations. Required data to develop LCI models for each mitigation strategy were collected from literature, databases, industry publications, catalogues, consultancy re- ports, and via personal consultation with domain experts in related companies and are summarized in the following sub sections. Tables reporting detailed LCI data are provided in Appendix A.

2.1.6.1.1. Feed input production. The primary inventory data for feed input production (corn, soybean, and wheat, which comprise the majority share of layer feed in Canada) were based on Pelletier (2017). The mitigation options considered were the application of 4Rs fertilizer management and biochar addition to the soil.

2.1.6.1.2. 4Rs fertilizer management. The inventory data for the 4Rs strategy were derived from (Li et al., 2016). Li et al. (2016) indicated that an 11% reduction in N fertilizer use is achievable while maintaining corn yield via a sensor-based variable-rate N application in Lincoln County, MO. The fertilizer flow in the baseline model (Pelletier 2017)

was modified based on Li et al. (2016) to represent the 4Rs approach. The 4Rs technique may also reduce the emissions of nitrous oxide, ammonia, and nitrate emissions in corn production by 10, 23, and 16%, respectively (Li et al., 2016). There was no literature found reporting the amount of fertilizer and emissions reduction associated with the appli- cation of the 4Rs approach in wheat and soybean production, specif- ically. Hence, the same% N fertilizer usage and emissions reduction that has been reported for corn was applied to the fertilizer usage and emissions for wheat and soybean production as calculated for the re- gions in which they produced in Canada.
The calculation of emissions reduction utilized Eq. (3): X = Z%) + Y (3)

<list>X= the amount of emission after application of mitigation option 
Y= the amount of emission before application of mitigation option 
Z= the percentage of reduction 
2.1.6.1.3. Biochar addition to soil. The primary data for biochar production were derived from Gu and Bergman (2016), who evaluated the co-production of biochar and syngas using a modular advanced biomass pyrolysis system in the US. The data were modified to best reflect Canadian conditions. The feedstock was assumed to be wood- chips from BC forests or mill residues. It was assumed that the biochar was produced in BC and shipped to the provinces producing the considered crops (corn, wheat, and soybean). 
Prior research has shown that the choice of system boundaries for biochar production models can significantly influence estimated life cycle impacts in particular with respect to allocation between syngas and biochar co-products. Consensus as to best practice has not yet coalesced (Matustík et al., 2020). Two scenarios were hence imple- mented in which biochar production was modeled using either physical allocation based on the energy content of biochar and syngas, or using a system expansion in which syngas was assumed to displace propane at a conversion rate of 78% (Ge et al., 2008). 
The application rate of biochar to agricultural soils depends on soil and crop type. According to literature, biochar application rates may vary from 5 to 50 tonnes per hectare (Chan et al., 2007; Major 2009, 2010). Some studies suggest that higher rates of biochar negatively affected crop yield. In the current study, a sensitivity analysis was conducted, and three rates of biochar addition (5, 7.5, and 10 tonnes per hectare) were modelled based on Dennis (2019). Due to the dependency of N emissions on application rate, type of soil, type of crop etc. (He et al., 2017) as well as lack of clarity with respect to the nature of this relationship (linear or otherwise), it was assumed that the lowest rate of biochar application modelled in this research (5 tonne/ha) has the lowest percentage of emission reduction from the range reported in Zargar et al. (2020). For example, biochar addition to the soil has been reported to reduce N2O emissions by 25-50%. Therefore, the following assumptions were made: 5, 7.5 and 10 tonne/ha biochar addition to the soil reduced N2O by 25%, 37.5%, and 50%, respectively. NO3 (0-25%) and NO (50-75%) emissions were reduced based on the same assump- tions. The amount of N20, NO, and NO3 in the baseline model, simulated using IPCC tier 2 protocols, were modified based on the above as- sumptions to reflect the anticipated impact of biochar application on N emissions. It was assumed biochar was incorporated into agricultural soils since the majority of biochar field trials reported used this appli- cation method (Major 2010). 
Biochar effects on soil and crop productivity cannot be generalized, as they depend on the type of biochar and plant as well as the site characteristics (Lorenz and Lal 2014). Jeffery et al. (2011) demonstrated that there is no correlation between the biochar application rate and the impacts on crop productivity. However, Gryze et al., 2010 found that biochar application reduced fertilizer application by 7% as a result of increased nutrient availability. Hence, a 7% fertilizer application rate reduction was considered when biochar was applied. 
Biochar is also advantageous in its potential for carbon sequestration that removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere as a result of storing</list>

carbon in soil organic matter. Windeatt et al. (2014) reported a potential of 373 Mt of biochar to sequester 0.55 Pg CO2 per year in soils over long-time periods. On this basis, it was assumed that 36 kg of biochar has an associated 53.01 kg annual carbon sequestration potential.

Another key feature of biochar is its resistance to decomposition in soils. This allows for a single application of biochar to be useful over several growing seasons (Major 2010). However, there is no recom- mended application frequency since research on biochar is relatively new/limited (Dennis 2019). The few studies that have been conducted to determine biochar application frequency best practices suggest application every three years (Backer et al., 2016; Bieser and Thomas 2019). Therefore, the frequency of application was considered to be three years in the current study as well.

The LCI data utilized to model feed input production with the application of the 4Rs approach and biochar addition to the soil are provided in Tables A.1 to A.3 for corn, soybean, and wheat production, respectively.

2.1.6.1.4. Feed formulation. Feed formulation is a critical and com- plex process to meet the nutritional requirements of laying hens so as to ensure productivity in a cost-effective manner (Moe et al., 2014). To evaluate the potential of improving NUE through feed formulation, a feed with reduced crude protein and supplemental amino acids was compared to a typical layer feed in Canada. Here, two representative formulations (a baseline and an alternative, reduced crude protein feed formulation with synthetic amino acids supplementation) were solicited from a western Canadian feed milling company (pers. comm, Alizadeh, 2019, Otter Coop) (Table A.4).

LCI models and source databases used to model ingredients in the feed formulations that were not otherwise provided in Pelletier (2017) are provided in Table A.5. Feed inputs affect nutrient excretion and associated emissions from manure; therefore, detailed feed formulations were provided in order to increase the transparency and accessibility of available data to other researchers to make comparisons among various diets. LCI data for DL-Methionine, L-lysine, L-threonine, monocalcium phosphate, and sodium bicarbonate were derived from AGRIBALYSE V1.3 2016), based on the project ECOALIM. Premix was modelled based on information provided by Otter Coop (pers. comm, Alizadeh, 2019, Otter Coop). Liquid choline was excluded from the analysis due to lack of reliable inventory data for the production process. As liquid choline is included at the same rate in both formulations, this omission is not anticipated to unduly influence the analysis.

2.1.6.1.5. Manure management. The manure management stage consists of three sub-stages: manure management in the barn, during storage, and at the time of land application. The baseline model (Pel- letier, 2017) consists of the technologies and management strategies that are already widely applied in the Canadian egg industry, some of which were shown in Zargar et al. (2020) to be effective in reducing N emissions compared to alternative strategies. These mitigation options include mechanical ventilation and use of manure belts in barns, use of roofed manure storage, and spreading the manure on land. However, other strategies were identified by Zargar et al. (2020), such as use of acid scrubbers at the barn stage, biochar addition to the stored manure, and incorporating manure at the time of land application. All were seen as potentially efficacious options to further reduce N emissions. Hence, a comparison among baseline versus alternative mitigation options were conducted at each sub stage of manure management. Specifically, for manure management in the barn, application of a manure belt and mechanical ventilation was considered as the baseline model, and an ammonia scrubber was applied as an additional, alternative option. Reported amounts of acid, electricity and water used in acid scrubbers, as well as the type of acid employed, vary considerably. As a result, a sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate and compare the extent to which the amount of inputs influences the estimated life cycle impacts. The most common type of acid used in the acid scrubber is sulfuric acid, which was considered in all models. Separate LCIs for acid scrubbers were considered based on (Hadlocon et al., 2015a), (Zhao et al., 2013),
and Zhang et al. (2016a), as well an average of these three studies. The results of the sensitivity analysis are provided in section 3.7.

It was assumed that the scrubber removed 75% of ammonia from ventilation air based on the low end of the range for ammonia removal efficiency (75-100%) reported in the literature (Carew 2010; Lin et al., 2014; Hadlocon et al. 2015; Wood and Van Heyst 2016). Based on an estimated N excretion rate by hens of 32.18 kg N/tonne of eggs and assuming 50% of ammonia emissions from the barn and storage occur in the barn, the total amount of N scrubbed was calculated accordingly. The captured NH3 was assumed to be made available as a fertilizer input to crop production, thereby displacing production of an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. LCI data for manure management in the barn with and without the application of a scrubber are provided in Appendix A.3 (LCI is provided in Table A.6).

At the manure storage stage, roofed storage was considered as the baseline model. Biochar addition to the stored manure was considered as an alternative strategy with the same LCI data as previously described for crop production. The rate of biochar application varies according to manure properties and feedstock materials (Steiner et al., 2010; Kalus et al., 2019). For the current analysis, a rate of 5% of poultry litter by mass was assumed based on (Steiner et al., 2010). LCI data for manure management in storage is provided in Table A.7. Biochar addition to manure reduced emissions (ammonia and nitrous oxide by the average amount of 62.5% and 87.5%, respectively), resulting in more N-rich manure at the time of land application.

At the manure land application stage, manure spreading was applied in the baseline model. Incorporating manure was considered as an alternative strategy. LCI data for manure land application is provided in Table A.8. Incorporation was assumed to be via chisel plow based on (Mark Hanna et al., 2014).

The previously described LCIs for the manure management stage were subsequently used in combination to model the potential combined effects of applying the strategies (Table A.9). The LCI models, including all background inventories as well as data quality scores, for these an- alyses are available as a Zolca file on the Open Science Framework at htt ps://osf.io/p2gmn/.

2.1.6.1.6. Egg production. LCI data, models, and source databases used to model production of one tonne of eggs assuming application of all mitigation strategies are provided in Table A.10. The feed production and manure management processes were modified, considering the application of the various strategies.

2.1.6.2. Modeling N P, and methane emissions from poultry manure in the barn, storage, and land application stages. 2.1.6.3. Uncertainty assessment. To quantify uncertainty related to sto- chastic variability and data quality, a pedigree matrix was applied using the Ecoinvent data quality system in openLCA. 2.1.6.3. Uncertainty assessment. To quantify uncertainty related to sto- chastic variability and data quality, a pedigree matrix was applied using the Ecoinvent data quality system in openLCA. Pedigree scores were applied to all foreground and background systems LCI data points. Monte Carlo (MC) simulations (1000 runs) were subsequently per- formed using openLCA software to quantify uncertainties for the LCIA results (standard error).
19: <header>2.1.6. Life cycle inventory analysis </header>

Foreground LCI model inputs and outputs, including direct, and in- direct emissions considered in this study included those attributable to the production and processing of feed inputs and other raw materials, energy carriers, and manure management from the baseline model, along with those specific to each of the mitigation options considered.

2.1.6.1. Data acquisition, assumptions, and limitations. Required data to develop LCI models for each mitigation strategy were collected from literature, databases, industry publications, catalogues, consultancy re- ports, and via personal consultation with domain experts in related companies and are summarized in the following sub sections. Tables reporting detailed LCI data are provided in Appendix A.

2.1.6.1.1. Feed input production. The primary inventory data for feed input production (corn, soybean, and wheat, which comprise the majority share of layer feed in Canada) were based on Pelletier (2017). The mitigation options considered were the application of 4Rs fertilizer management and biochar addition to the soil.

2.1.6.1.2. 4Rs fertilizer management. The inventory data for the 4Rs strategy were derived from (Li et al., 2016). Li et al. (2016) indicated that an 11% reduction in N fertilizer use is achievable while maintaining corn yield via a sensor-based variable-rate N application in Lincoln County, MO. The fertilizer flow in the baseline model (Pelletier 2017)

was modified based on Li et al. (2016) to represent the 4Rs approach. The 4Rs technique may also reduce the emissions of nitrous oxide, ammonia, and nitrate emissions in corn production by 10, 23, and 16%, respectively (Li et al., 2016). There was no literature found reporting the amount of fertilizer and emissions reduction associated with the appli- cation of the 4Rs approach in wheat and soybean production, specif- ically. Hence, the same% N fertilizer usage and emissions reduction that has been reported for corn was applied to the fertilizer usage and emissions for wheat and soybean production as calculated for the re- gions in which they produced in Canada.
The calculation of emissions reduction utilized Eq. (3): X = Z%) + Y (3)

<list>X= the amount of emission after application of mitigation option 
Y= the amount of emission before application of mitigation option 
Z= the percentage of reduction 
2.1.6.1.3. Biochar addition to soil. The primary data for biochar production were derived from Gu and Bergman (2016), who evaluated the co-production of biochar and syngas using a modular advanced biomass pyrolysis system in the US. The data were modified to best reflect Canadian conditions. The feedstock was assumed to be wood- chips from BC forests or mill residues. It was assumed that the biochar was produced in BC and shipped to the provinces producing the considered crops (corn, wheat, and soybean). 
Prior research has shown that the choice of system boundaries for biochar production models can significantly influence estimated life cycle impacts in particular with respect to allocation between syngas and biochar co-products. Consensus as to best practice has not yet coalesced (Matustík et al., 2020). Two scenarios were hence imple- mented in which biochar production was modeled using either physical allocation based on the energy content of biochar and syngas, or using a system expansion in which syngas was assumed to displace propane at a conversion rate of 78% (Ge et al., 2008). 
The application rate of biochar to agricultural soils depends on soil and crop type. According to literature, biochar application rates may vary from 5 to 50 tonnes per hectare (Chan et al., 2007; Major 2009, 2010). Some studies suggest that higher rates of biochar negatively affected crop yield. In the current study, a sensitivity analysis was conducted, and three rates of biochar addition (5, 7.5, and 10 tonnes per hectare) were modelled based on Dennis (2019). Due to the dependency of N emissions on application rate, type of soil, type of crop etc. (He et al., 2017) as well as lack of clarity with respect to the nature of this relationship (linear or otherwise), it was assumed that the lowest rate of biochar application modelled in this research (5 tonne/ha) has the lowest percentage of emission reduction from the range reported in Zargar et al. (2020). For example, biochar addition to the soil has been reported to reduce N2O emissions by 25-50%. Therefore, the following assumptions were made: 5, 7.5 and 10 tonne/ha biochar addition to the soil reduced N2O by 25%, 37.5%, and 50%, respectively. NO3 (0-25%) and NO (50-75%) emissions were reduced based on the same assump- tions. The amount of N20, NO, and NO3 in the baseline model, simulated using IPCC tier 2 protocols, were modified based on the above as- sumptions to reflect the anticipated impact of biochar application on N emissions. It was assumed biochar was incorporated into agricultural soils since the majority of biochar field trials reported used this appli- cation method (Major 2010). 
Biochar effects on soil and crop productivity cannot be generalized, as they depend on the type of biochar and plant as well as the site characteristics (Lorenz and Lal 2014). Jeffery et al. (2011) demonstrated that there is no correlation between the biochar application rate and the impacts on crop productivity. However, Gryze et al., 2010 found that biochar application reduced fertilizer application by 7% as a result of increased nutrient availability. Hence, a 7% fertilizer application rate reduction was considered when biochar was applied. 
Biochar is also advantageous in its potential for carbon sequestration that removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere as a result of storing</list>

carbon in soil organic matter. Windeatt et al. (2014) reported a potential of 373 Mt of biochar to sequester 0.55 Pg CO2 per year in soils over long-time periods. On this basis, it was assumed that 36 kg of biochar has an associated 53.01 kg annual carbon sequestration potential.

Another key feature of biochar is its resistance to decomposition in soils. This allows for a single application of biochar to be useful over several growing seasons (Major 2010). However, there is no recom- mended application frequency since research on biochar is relatively new/limited (Dennis 2019). The few studies that have been conducted to determine biochar application frequency best practices suggest application every three years (Backer et al., 2016; Bieser and Thomas 2019). Therefore, the frequency of application was considered to be three years in the current study as well.

The LCI data utilized to model feed input production with the application of the 4Rs approach and biochar addition to the soil are provided in Tables A.1 to A.3 for corn, soybean, and wheat production, respectively.

2.1.6.1.4. Feed formulation. Feed formulation is a critical and com- plex process to meet the nutritional requirements of laying hens so as to ensure productivity in a cost-effective manner (Moe et al., 2014). To evaluate the potential of improving NUE through feed formulation, a feed with reduced crude protein and supplemental amino acids was compared to a typical layer feed in Canada. Here, two representative formulations (a baseline and an alternative, reduced crude protein feed formulation with synthetic amino acids supplementation) were solicited from a western Canadian feed milling company (pers. comm, Alizadeh, 2019, Otter Coop) (Table A.4).

LCI models and source databases used to model ingredients in the feed formulations that were not otherwise provided in Pelletier (2017) are provided in Table A.5. Feed inputs affect nutrient excretion and associated emissions from manure; therefore, detailed feed formulations were provided in order to increase the transparency and accessibility of available data to other researchers to make comparisons among various diets. LCI data for DL-Methionine, L-lysine, L-threonine, monocalcium phosphate, and sodium bicarbonate were derived from AGRIBALYSE V1.3 2016), based on the project ECOALIM. Premix was modelled based on information provided by Otter Coop (pers. comm, Alizadeh, 2019, Otter Coop). Liquid choline was excluded from the analysis due to lack of reliable inventory data for the production process. As liquid choline is included at the same rate in both formulations, this omission is not anticipated to unduly influence the analysis.

2.1.6.1.5. Manure management. The manure management stage consists of three sub-stages: manure management in the barn, during storage, and at the time of land application. The baseline model (Pel- letier, 2017) consists of the technologies and management strategies that are already widely applied in the Canadian egg industry, some of which were shown in Zargar et al. (2020) to be effective in reducing N emissions compared to alternative strategies. These mitigation options include mechanical ventilation and use of manure belts in barns, use of roofed manure storage, and spreading the manure on land. However, other strategies were identified by Zargar et al. (2020), such as use of acid scrubbers at the barn stage, biochar addition to the stored manure, and incorporating manure at the time of land application. All were seen as potentially efficacious options to further reduce N emissions. Hence, a comparison among baseline versus alternative mitigation options were conducted at each sub stage of manure management. Specifically, for manure management in the barn, application of a manure belt and mechanical ventilation was considered as the baseline model, and an ammonia scrubber was applied as an additional, alternative option. Reported amounts of acid, electricity and water used in acid scrubbers, as well as the type of acid employed, vary considerably. As a result, a sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate and compare the extent to which the amount of inputs influences the estimated life cycle impacts. The most common type of acid used in the acid scrubber is sulfuric acid, which was considered in all models. Separate LCIs for acid scrubbers were considered based on (Hadlocon et al., 2015a), (Zhao et al., 2013),
and Zhang et al. (2016a), as well an average of these three studies. The results of the sensitivity analysis are provided in section 3.7.

It was assumed that the scrubber removed 75% of ammonia from ventilation air based on the low end of the range for ammonia removal efficiency (75-100%) reported in the literature (Carew 2010; Lin et al., 2014; Hadlocon et al. 2015; Wood and Van Heyst 2016). Based on an estimated N excretion rate by hens of 32.18 kg N/tonne of eggs and assuming 50% of ammonia emissions from the barn and storage occur in the barn, the total amount of N scrubbed was calculated accordingly. The captured NH3 was assumed to be made available as a fertilizer input to crop production, thereby displacing production of an equivalent amount of N fertilizer. LCI data for manure management in the barn with and without the application of a scrubber are provided in Appendix A.3 (LCI is provided in Table A.6).

At the manure storage stage, roofed storage was considered as the baseline model. Biochar addition to the stored manure was considered as an alternative strategy with the same LCI data as previously described for crop production. The rate of biochar application varies according to manure properties and feedstock materials (Steiner et al., 2010; Kalus et al., 2019). For the current analysis, a rate of 5% of poultry litter by mass was assumed based on (Steiner et al., 2010). LCI data for manure management in storage is provided in Table A.7. Biochar addition to manure reduced emissions (ammonia and nitrous oxide by the average amount of 62.5% and 87.5%, respectively), resulting in more N-rich manure at the time of land application.

At the manure land application stage, manure spreading was applied in the baseline model. Incorporating manure was considered as an alternative strategy. LCI data for manure land application is provided in Table A.8. Incorporation was assumed to be via chisel plow based on (Mark Hanna et al., 2014).

The previously described LCIs for the manure management stage were subsequently used in combination to model the potential combined effects of applying the strategies (Table A.9). The LCI models, including all background inventories as well as data quality scores, for these an- alyses are available as a Zolca file on the Open Science Framework at htt ps://osf.io/p2gmn/.

2.1.6.1.6. Egg production. LCI data, models, and source databases used to model production of one tonne of eggs assuming application of all mitigation strategies are provided in Table A.10. The feed production and manure management processes were modified, considering the application of the various strategies.

2.1.6.2. Modeling N P, and methane emissions from poultry manure in the barn, storage, and land application stages. The application of suitable emissions models is crucial to increase the reliability of LCIA results (Zargar submitted). Here, suitable emissions models for estimating N emissions were determined based on (Zargar et al., submitted), which reviewed, characterized, and compared publicly available N emission models for use in LCA. Among the 48 models reviewed, IPCC Tier 2 emission models were identified as the most suitable option for the current study, due to their capacity to support simulating nitrous oxide, ammonia, nitrate, and nitric oxide emissions at the national scale (which is consistent with the application context of the current study). The IPCC methods are intended to support quantification of GHG emissions, including from both direct and indirect emissions of nitrous oxide. In- direct sources of nitrous oxide emissions occur via ammonia, nitrate, and nitric oxide volatilization, deposition, leaching and runoff. Hence, the IPCC methods also provide methods and emission factors for calculating emissions of ammonia, nitrate, and nitric oxide (IPCC, 2006), which were used in the current analysis.

The rate of nitrogen excretion by poultry is required to calculate the amount of emissions in the barn, storage, and land application stages of manure management. Nitrogen excretion rates were calculated as described in Pelletier (2017) via a nutrient mass balance model, with feed input nitrogen contents derived from www.feedipedia.org. The

proportion of emitted nitrous oxide, ammonia and nitric oxide from the nitrogen excreted in barn VS. storage stages is unclear. IPCC Tier 2 provides emission factors for the combination of these two stages only. To consider barn and manure storage stages separately, it was assumed that emissions were divided equally between these two stages (i.e. 50% of total emissions were attributed to the barn stage and the other 50% to the storage stage). A sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate the influence of this assumption on model results assuming a 25%/75% split, and vice versa.

Emissions from manure land application were assessed using IPCC (2006) Tier 2 default emission factors. The phosphorus excretion rate was calculated via a nutrient mass balance. Phosphorus emissions were calculated based on an assumed 2.9% run-off rate at the time of manure application (Dalgaard et al., 2008). Methane emissions were assumed to be emitted 100% in manure storage, and were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 2 methods.

2.1.6.3. Uncertainty assessment. To quantify uncertainty related to sto- chastic variability and data quality, a pedigree matrix was applied using the Ecoinvent data quality system in openLCA. Pedigree scores were applied to all foreground and background systems LCI data points. Monte Carlo (MC) simulations (1000 runs) were subsequently per- formed using openLCA software to quantify uncertainties for the LCIA results (standard error).

The 4Rs technique may also reduce the emissions of nitrous oxide, ammonia, and nitrate emissions in corn production by 10, 23, and 16%, respectively (Li et al., 2016). There was no literature found reporting the amount of fertilizer and emissions reduction associated with the appli- cation of the 4Rs approach in wheat and soybean production, specif- ically.
