44: <header>3.5. Life cycle impacts of NUE strategies for manure management: Field application </header>

NUE, N footprint, N input and outputs associated with manure land application are presented in Table B.5. Incorporation had 4% higher NUE and reduced the N footprint by 17% compared to the baseline scenario. The results were due to the effects of incorporation on ammonia emissions. This finding is consistent with that of Smith et al. (2009) and Webb et al. (2005), who indicated incorporation was the best ammonia mitigation option compared to surface application. However, nitrous oxide emissions increased with this strategy. The reported effects of manure incorporation on N2O loss are mixed. In conditions with intense denitrification, incorporation reduced N2O emissions and vice versa (Thorman and Chadwick 2008; Chadwick et al., 2011). NUE and
LCIA results are provided in Table 4 and Figure C.7.

Compared to the baseline strategy (surface application only), manure incorporation resulted in lower impacts in the acidification and eutrophication categories (65%, and 15%). However, it considerably increased global warming (138%) and slightly increased energy con- sumption (5%) compared to manure spreading due to higher nitrous oxide emissions and additional fuel consumption. Manure land appli- cation was found to be one of the main contributors to global warming emissions from swine manure management (Zhong et al., 2013). No information was found regarding the effects of different manure land application techniques from a life cycle perspective.

46: <header>3.6. Life cycle impacts of NUE strategies for the egg supply chain </header>

NUE and N footprints per tonne of eggs produced under the baseline and alternative scenarios are shown in Table C.7. In this study, life cycle NUE for egg production for the baseline scenario was 67%, and ranged from 59% to 82%, depending on the alternative strategy applied. Ac- cording to Hristov et al. (2006), Powell et al. (2010), and Gourley et al. (2012b), whole dairy farm NUE varied from 8% to 64%, and is impacted by a range of farming practices. It is worth noting that NUE for egg and chicken production is generally higher than for milk production (Groe- nestein et al., 2019).

The largest gain in NUE among scenarios was achieved by the application of all strategies combined (82%), followed by the use of the scrubber (80%). The application of all strategies and scrubber applica- tion in the barn showed 15% and 13% higher NUE compared to the baseline scenario. Implementation of all strategies resulted in the lowest N footprint, followed by scrubber use. This was, in part, due to the ni- trogen credit from the acid scrubber. The reduced crude protein diet supplemented with synthetic amino acids decreased life cycle NUE to 59% per tonne of eggs and increased the N footprint considerably compared to the baseline scenario, despite generating significant improvement in NUE at the feed formulation stage due to the lower NUE of the substituted feed inputs. NUE and LCIA results for the baseline scenario compared to the alternative scenarios are provided in Table 5 and Figure C.8. In addition, a comparative LCIA of one tonne of eggs produced, including reference and alternative scenarios is shown in Fig. 2. All impact category results are scaled relative to the reference scenario results.
For acidification potential, the application of all strategies reduced impacts by 60% and 35% when considering biochar production modelled using energy allocation and system expansion, respectively.

Fig. 2. Comparative LCIA results for 1 tonne of eggs produced relative to the baseline.

Application of an acid scrubber had the second-largest SO2 reduction (21%) followed by biochar addition to the stored manure. The latter resulted in a 12% (energy allocation) to 14% (system expansion) reduction. The most significant contributor to acidification potential was ammonia emissions, followed by sulfur dioxide emissions.

For GWP, the application of all strategies showed a large reduction in global warming, regardless of whether allocation or system expansion was applied in the biochar model. However, the reduction was more prominent for system expansion (406% lower than the baseline) as compared to allocation (76% lower than the baseline). Both biochar addition to the soil and to the manure showed a significant reduction in estimated GHG emissions. In contrast, incorporating manure increased CO2 emissions by 5% compared to the baseline. This is because of the higher fuel consumption needed to incorporate manure as well as increased N2O emissions. The global warming potential (1531.87 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of eggs) was found to be much lower than has been reported in other studies such as Ghasempour and Ahmadi (2016) (4070 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of eggs in Iran), and Dekker et al. (2008) (4000 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of organic eggs and 2470 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of conventional eggs in the Netherlands.)

For depletion of abiotic resources, the application of the 4Rs approach made only a small difference (1% reduction) compared with the baseline scenario. However, since the application of each of the other mitigation options increased life cycle fossil fuel consumption, the alternative scenarios resulted in higher abiotic depletion compared to the baseline scenario. Only biochar application showed marked benefits with respect to net abiotic depletion potential under the system expan- sion scenario.

For the energy use category, the 4Rs strategy showed an insignificant reduction (only 1%) due to the lower N fertilizer input. All other alter- native scenarios resulted in higher energy use. Biochar production was either the main source of energy consumption (allocation) or net cu- mulative energy demand benefits (system expansion). The reduced crude protein diet increased the cumulative energy demand of egg production considerably (i.e. by 26% compared to the control diet), while application of the scrubber increased life cycle energy use by 5%.

For eutrophication potential, application of all strategies resulted in the lowest impact, regardless of whether energy allocation or system expansion was applied in the biochar model. The second highest eutrophication potential reduction was associated with use of the acid scrubber (16% reduction). The main contributors to this impact category were ammonia and nitrate emissions, followed by phosphate emissions. The reduced crude protein diet scenario reduced eutrophying impacts by 10% compared to the baseline.

For ozone layer depletion potential, the amount of CFC-11 equiva- lent was very small for the baseline scenario (0.11 g CFC-11 eq.). Hence, no differences were observed when the 4Rs approach, acid scrubber, and manure incorporation were implemented. The emissions were more significant in scenarios using gas and petroleum, which resulted in methane emissions. The reduced crude protein diet scenario increased life cycle ozone depleting emissions by 9% compared to the baseline scenario, due to the greater use of pesticides and machinery for the production of wheat in the diet, as well as the synthetic amino acids production.

For the photochemical oxidation impact category, the impacts of the various strategies on emissions were insignificant. Application of the reduced crude protein diet showed a 5% emissions reduction. On the other hand, the application of the scrubber increased emissions by 3%. The most relevant contributors were emissions of carbon monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO2), nitrogen oxide (NO), ammonium and non- methane volatile organic compounds.

addition to theaddition to the crude protein
Fig. 2. Comparative LCIA results for 1 tonne of eggs produced relative to the baseline.

Application of an acid scrubber had the second-largest SO2 reduction (21%) followed by biochar addition to the stored manure. The latter resulted in a 12% (energy allocation) to 14% (system expansion) reduction. The most significant contributor to acidification potential was ammonia emissions, followed by sulfur dioxide emissions.

For GWP, the application of all strategies showed a large reduction in global warming, regardless of whether allocation or system expansion was applied in the biochar model. However, the reduction was more prominent for system expansion (406% lower than the baseline) as compared to allocation (76% lower than the baseline). Both biochar addition to the soil and to the manure showed a significant reduction in estimated GHG emissions. In contrast, incorporating manure increased CO2 emissions by 5% compared to the baseline. This is because of the higher fuel consumption needed to incorporate manure as well as increased N2O emissions. The global warming potential (1531.87 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of eggs) was found to be much lower than has been reported in other studies such as Ghasempour and Ahmadi (2016) (4070 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of eggs in Iran), and Dekker et al. (2008) (4000 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of organic eggs and 2470 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of conventional eggs in the Netherlands.)

For depletion of abiotic resources, the application of the 4Rs approach made only a small difference (1% reduction) compared with the baseline scenario. However, since the application of each of the other mitigation options increased life cycle fossil fuel consumption, the alternative scenarios resulted in higher abiotic depletion compared to the baseline scenario. Only biochar application showed marked benefits with respect to net abiotic depletion potential under the system expan- sion scenario.

For the energy use category, the 4Rs strategy showed an insignificant reduction (only 1%) due to the lower N fertilizer input. All other alter- native scenarios resulted in higher energy use. Biochar production was either the main source of energy consumption (allocation) or net cu- mulative energy demand benefits (system expansion). The reduced crude protein diet increased the cumulative energy demand of egg production considerably (i.e. by 26% compared to the control diet), while application of the scrubber increased life cycle energy use by 5%.

For eutrophication potential, application of all strategies resulted in the lowest impact, regardless of whether energy allocation or system expansion was applied in the biochar model. The second highest eutrophication potential reduction was associated with use of the acid scrubber (16% reduction). The main contributors to this impact category were ammonia and nitrate emissions, followed by phosphate emissions. The reduced crude protein diet scenario reduced eutrophying impacts by 10% compared to the baseline.

For ozone layer depletion potential, the amount of CFC-11 equiva- lent was very small for the baseline scenario (0.11 g CFC-11 eq.). Hence, no differences were observed when the 4Rs approach, acid scrubber, and manure incorporation were implemented. The emissions were more significant in scenarios using gas and petroleum, which resulted in methane emissions. The reduced crude protein diet scenario increased life cycle ozone depleting emissions by 9% compared to the baseline scenario, due to the greater use of pesticides and machinery for the production of wheat in the diet, as well as the synthetic amino acids production.

For the photochemical oxidation impact category, the impacts of the various strategies on emissions were insignificant. Application of the reduced crude protein diet showed a 5% emissions reduction. On the other hand, the application of the scrubber increased emissions by 3%. The most relevant contributors were emissions of carbon monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO2), nitrogen oxide (NO), ammonium and non- methane volatile organic compounds.

The latter resulted in a 12% (energy allocation) to 14% (system expansion) reduction. The most significant contributor to acidification potential was ammonia emissions, followed by sulfur dioxide emissions.

For GWP, the application of all strategies showed a large reduction in global warming, regardless of whether allocation or system expansion was applied in the biochar model. However, the reduction was more prominent for system expansion (406% lower than the baseline) as compared to allocation (76% lower than the baseline). Both biochar addition to the soil and to the manure showed a significant reduction in estimated GHG emissions. In contrast, incorporating manure increased CO2 emissions by 5% compared to the baseline. This is because of the higher fuel consumption needed to incorporate manure as well as increased N2O emissions. The global warming potential (1531.87 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of eggs) was found to be much lower than has been reported in other studies such as Ghasempour and Ahmadi (2016) (4070 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of eggs in Iran), and Dekker et al. (2008) (4000 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of organic eggs and 2470 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of conventional eggs in the Netherlands.)

For depletion of abiotic resources, the application of the 4Rs approach made only a small difference (1% reduction) compared with the baseline scenario. However, since the application of each of the other mitigation options increased life cycle fossil fuel consumption, the alternative scenarios resulted in higher abiotic depletion compared to the baseline scenario. Only biochar application showed marked benefits with respect to net abiotic depletion potential under the system expan- sion scenario.

For the energy use category, the 4Rs strategy showed an insignificant reduction (only 1%) due to the lower N fertilizer input. All other alter- native scenarios resulted in higher energy use. Biochar production was either the main source of energy consumption (allocation) or net cu- mulative energy demand benefits (system expansion). The reduced crude protein diet increased the cumulative energy demand of egg production considerably (i.e. by 26% compared to the control diet), while application of the scrubber increased life cycle energy use by 5%.

For eutrophication potential, application of all strategies resulted in the lowest impact, regardless of whether energy allocation or system expansion was applied in the biochar model. The second highest eutrophication potential reduction was associated with use of the acid scrubber (16% reduction). The main contributors to this impact category were ammonia and nitrate emissions, followed by phosphate emissions. The reduced crude protein diet scenario reduced eutrophying impacts by 10% compared to the baseline.

For ozone layer depletion potential, the amount of CFC-11 equiva- lent was very small for the baseline scenario (0.11 g CFC-11 eq.). Hence, no differences were observed when the 4Rs approach, acid scrubber, and manure incorporation were implemented. The emissions were more significant in scenarios using gas and petroleum, which resulted in methane emissions. The reduced crude protein diet scenario increased life cycle ozone depleting emissions by 9% compared to the baseline scenario, due to the greater use of pesticides and machinery for the production of wheat in the diet, as well as the synthetic amino acids production.

For the photochemical oxidation impact category, the impacts of the various strategies on emissions were insignificant. Application of the reduced crude protein diet showed a 5% emissions reduction. On the other hand, the application of the scrubber increased emissions by 3%. The most relevant contributors were emissions of carbon monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO2), nitrogen oxide (NO), ammonium and non- methane volatile organic compounds.
59: Sensitivity analyses were conducted for (1) the rate of biochar addition to the soil, (2) the emission factors applied in the barn and
manure storage stages, and (3) the amount of acid, electricity, and water required per unit of ammonia scrubbed.
60: <header>3.7.1. Rate of biochar addition to soil </header>

The literature indicates a wide range of potential biochar addition rates to agricultural soil. LCIA results for feed input production assuming biochar application rates of 5, 7.5, and 10 tonnes ha-1 are provided in APPENDIX D (Table D.1). The lowest impacts derived from the appli- cation of 5 tonnes ha-1 in all impact categories, under the energy allo- cation scenario. Higher application rates affected all impact categories negatively, except GWP. The impact was most pronounced in the energy use and climate change category.

In corn production, energy use was 39% and 79% higher for 7.5 and 10 tonnes ha-1 biochar addition compared to the 5 tonne ha-1 biochar addition scenario. Impacts were similarly higher for ozone layer deple- tion (25% and 50%) and photochemical oxidation (31% and 62%). The higher rates of application did not increase emissions in the acidification and eutrophication impact categories substantially, mainly due to the potential of biochar to reduce N emissions at the higher rates. The effects of greater application rates were more significant in soybean and wheat production compared to corn production, mainly due to the larger land area that was requiring more biochar and energy consumption for bio- char land application.

62: <header>3.7.2. Emission factors applied in the barn and manure storage stages </header>

The IPCC Tier 2 emissions models provide combined EFs for manure in barn and storage. To estimate emissions at each stage separately, it was assumed that 50% of emissions occur at each of the barn and storage stages. LCIA results for egg production assuming a 25%/75% and 75%/ 25% split in emissions between the barn and storage stages compared to the baseline assumption (50% barn, 50% storage) are provided in AP- PENDIX D (Table D.2). Differences in the assumed distribution of emissions between the barn and storage stages resulted in no differences in estimated impacts for some impact categories such as the depletion of fossil fuels, and ozone layer depletion.

The result showed relatively minor differences, (2% to 3%), in acidification, climate change, energy use, and eutrophication categories. Assuming 25% of emissions occur in the barn increased the above emissions by a maximum of 3%. Moreover, assuming 75% of emissions in the barn reduced emissions by 2%. Assuming more emissions in the barn supported more emission reduction via the scrubber, as well as a larger nitrogen credit since more ammonia is captured.
63: <header>3.7.3. Acid scrubber: Acid, water, and electricity usage </header>

The reported amount of acid, electricity, and water required per unit of ammonia scrubbed varies widely in the literature. In order to evaluate and compare the extent to which the amount of inputs influence esti- mated emissions, a sensitivity analysis was conducted.

According to Zhang et al. (2016a), for every kg of ammonia scrub- bed, the average water, sodium bisulfate and electricity consumption were 0.23 15.10 kg and 43.74 kWh, respectively. However, Hadlocon et al. (2015) reported much lower inputs at 380 cm³ water, 0.03 kg sulfuric acid and 0.28 kWh electricity per kg of ammonia scrubbed. Based on data from Zhao (2013), an average of 510 cm³ water, 0.04 kg sulfuric acid and 0.01 kWh electricity are required. LCIA results based on each of these input levels, as well as the average data, used in the previously described results are shown in APPENDIX D (Table D.3).

The results indicated substantially lower emissions all across impact categories when using data from both Hadlocon et al. (2015) and Zhao (2013) compared to those calculated using the Zhang et al. (2016a) data. The lower impacts in the abiotic depletion fuels, energy use and global warming categories were mainly due to lower electricity consumption. The decreased ozone layer depletion and photochemical oxidation impacts were influenced by lesser acid consumption. In light of the large differences in estimated impacts, and the potential utility of acid scrubbers as an NUE efficiency strategy for egg supply chains,
development of improved LCI data for acid scrubbers should be considered a research priority in the context of egg industry sustain- ability research.

65: The uncertainty analysis results for all estimated life cycle impacts in each scenario are provided in APPENDIX E. In addition, Figures include error bars (standard error).

At the feed input production stage, the differences between the baseline and the alternative scenarios (4Rs approach and biochar addition to the soil) were statistically significant (p < 0.05) for all impact categories for corn production. However, for climate change impacts, the difference was not statistically significant between the baseline and biochar addition scenario, when the system expansion approach was adopted. For soybean production, the differences between the baseline and 4Rs application were not statistically significant across impact categories. However, the differences were statistically significant be- tween the baseline and biochar addition, regardless of whether energy allocation or system expansion was used in the biochar production model. For wheat production, most differences between the baseline and alternative strategies were statistically significant except for climate change, eutrophication, and photochemical oxidation when the alloca- tion approach was applied.

At the feed formulation stage, as well as the manure management in the barn and on the field, the differences between the baseline scenario and NUE strategies were statistically significant for all impact cate- gories. At the manure storage stage, the differences between the baseline and biochar addition to the stored manure (both allocation and system expansion approaches) were not statistically significant for the acidifi- cation and eutrophication impact categories. The climate change impact category results were also not significantly different when the system expansion approach was applied in the biochar production model.

With respect to the life cycle impacts of egg production, the differ- ences between the reference scenario and 4Rs application were not statistically significant for the acidification, eutrophication, and photo- chemical oxidation categories. Uncertainty analysis showed significant differences in all impact categories between the reference and biochar addition to soil scenarios when system expansion was utilized, but not when energy allocation was utilized.

Differences were statistically significant for most impact categories (except photochemical oxidation) between the reference scenario and the following NUE mitigation strategies: reduced crude protein diet, application of an acid scrubber in the barn, and the biochar addition to the stored manure (except for ozone layer depletion when system expansion was considered). At the manure land application stage, the differences between spreading (reference scenario) and incorporation (the alternative strategy) were statistically significant for all categories.

Simultaneous application of all strategies had statistically significant differences in results compared to the reference scenario in all categories when system expansion for biochar addition was considered. However, the differences were not statistically significant for climate change, eutrophication, or photochemical oxidation when energy allocation was applied in the biochar production model.

The uncertainties were largely associated with the background data quality indicators as quantified using the pedigree matrix. The back- ground inventories applied in this study were mostly from Ecoinvent, USDA, or were created based on the literature. Many of these inventories are representative of European and American rather than Canadian conditions, which, in turn decreases the certainty of model outcomes. On-going development of the Canadian Agri-food Life Cycle Data centre (CALDC) will reduce such model uncertainties in the future.

69: The top three strategies that might be applied along egg supply
chains in terms of reducing environmental impacts and nitrogen foot- prints while increasing NUE are summarized in Table 6 (energy allo- cation is considered in the biochar models).

The results clearly indicate that each strategy can be considered more or less suitable depending on the environmental impacts of concern, as well as NUE outcomes. For example, scrubber application showed a 13% higher NUE and reduced the N footprint by 24% compared to the baseline. It also resulted in lower acidification and eutrophication impacts (21% and 16%). However, this scenario pro- duced higher impacts (2% - 5%) with respect to abiotic depletion, en- ergy use, and photochemical oxidation. The 4Rs strategy was the only option that improved outcomes in all impact categories, but these im- provements were quite small. In addition, improvements in NUE (4%) and the nitrogen footprint (4%) were relatively modest. Combined application of all strategies achieved the best outcomes with respect to global warming, acidification, and eutrophication potential (76%, 35% and 26% less impact than baseline, respectively), but at the expense of a large increase in energy use (by 401%), ozone layer depletion (by 164%), and abiotic depletion (by 195%), compared to the baseline. However, when using system expansion in the biochar production model, the relevant scenarios, had the lowest emissions in most impact categories.

71: The combined application of LCA with NUE and N footprint calcu- lations enabled the understanding of opportunities and constraints for improving outcomes via application of nitrogen emissions mitigation technologies and management strategies. It can be concluded that NUE and N footprints should not be considered in isolation when choosing amongst alternative options. Rather, it is imperative to consider such interventions using life cycle models as well as taking into account multiple relevant resource and environmental indicators so as to be aware of and manage for trade-offs.

Mitigation options considered at each stage of egg supply chains generally improved NUE with respect to the specific activities consid- ered. However, this did not always hold true when considering nitrogen efficiency for the entire egg production supply chain. For example, the reduced crude protein diet decreased nitrogen excretion per tonne of eggs produced, but decreased systems-level N efficiency due to the lower NUE efficiencies associated with inputs to the reduced crude protein
72: Top strategies for egg supply chains for reducing environmental impacts and nitrogen footprints and improving NUE.

Impact category|First|Second|Third Acidification potential|(S5) An acid scrubber application|(S6) Biochar addition to the manure|(S4) Reduced crude protein diet GWP|(S3) Biochar addition to the soil|(S6) Biochar addition to the manure|(S2) Application of 4Rs strategy Depletion of abiotic|(S2) 4Rs approach|(S1) Baseline|None Energy use|(S2) 4Rs approach|(S1) Baseline|None Eutrophication|(S5) An acid scrubber application|(S6) Biochar addition to the manure|(S2) 4Rs approach Ozone layer depletion|(S1) Baseline|(S2), (S5) or (S7)|None Photochemical oxidation|(S4) Reduced crude protein diet|(S1) Baseline|(S2) or (S7) NUE|(S5) An acid scrubber application|(S6) Biochar addition to the manure|(S2) 4Rs approach N footprint|(S5) An acid scrubber application|(S4) Reduced crude protein diet|(S6) Biochar addition to the manure
73: Overall, application of all strategies as well as an acid scrubber appear to provide the most efficacious options with respect to NUE improvement (15% and 13%, respectively). These strategies reduce acidification (35% and 21%) and eutrophication potential (26% and 16%), but increase energy consumption, abiotic depletion and ozone layer depletion. Use of renewable energy to support these activities would result in win-win outcomes across impact categories. To further support optimizing egg production supply chains, the following studies may be helpful. First, farm-specific LCAs are desirable to increase the rigor of the analyses through improved accuracy of input data. Second, research regarding the effects of the 4Rs approach on various crops is required. Third, a more detailed analysis of the reduced crude protein diet strategy is warranted, since the impacts of feed formulation depend strongly on the percentage and types of ingredients utilized. Our anal- ysis addressed two representative examples only. Despite that the alternative, reduced crude protein diet was not efficacious in improving NUE, it is essential to underscore that other formulations may be much more effective, depending on the upstream impacts of producing the specific feed inputs. Fourth, considering different types of scrubbers as well as developing improved LCI data for scrubbers should be prioritized in light of the considerable mitigation potential of this technology and heterogeneity in reported inputs. Finally, carrying out detailed eco- nomic and social sustainability assessments are desirable to support informed decision making regarding NUE strategies.
